stem: The cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) has order \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \). How many of its elements are generators (that is, have order exactly [[nval]])?
solution: The number of generators of \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) is Euler's totient \( \varphi(n) \). For \( n = p^{a} q^{b} \), \( \varphi(n) = p^{a-1}(p-1)\,q^{b-1}(q-1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( (p-1)(q-1) \), dropping the prime-power factors \( p^{a-1} \) and \( q^{b-1} \).
This keeps only \( p^{a-1}q^{b-1} \) and forgets the \( (p-1)(q-1) \) factors.
This counts every non-identity element as a generator, which is false unless the order is prime.
This applies an inclusion-exclusion by the wrong terms.
This is off by one from \( \varphi(n) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi(n) \).
This is off by two from \( \varphi(n) \).

stem: A cyclic group \( G \) has PRIME order [[p]]. Since the only positive divisors of [[p]] are 1 and [[p]], every non-identity element must have order [[p]]. How many generators does \( G \) have?
solution: In a group of prime order [[p]], every element except the identity generates the whole group, so the number of generators is \( [[p]] - 1 \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly includes the identity element as a generator.
This overcounts by one beyond the group's own order.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This mistakenly halves the count.
This mistakenly doubles the count.
This just restates \( p \), not \( p - 1 \).

stem: A cyclic group \( G \) has prime-power order \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[k]]} \). Unlike a group of prime order, not every non-identity element generates \( G \) here (an element generates iff its order is the full \( p^{k} \), i.e., it is not a multiple of \( p \)). How many generators does \( G \) have?
solution: An element of \( \mathbb{Z}_{p^{k}} \) generates the group iff it is not divisible by \( p \); there are \( p^{k-1} \) multiples of \( p \) in \( \{0,\dots,p^{k}-1\} \), so the generator count is \( p^{k} - p^{k-1} = p^{k-1}(p-1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats every non-identity element as a generator, which is only valid for prime (not prime-power) order.
This reports the number of NON-generators (multiples of \( p \)), not the generators.
This subtracts in the wrong direction.
This uses \( p - 1 \) alone, dropping the \( p^{k-1} \) scaling factor.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: Consider the direct product \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), where \( \gcd([[m]], [[n]]) = 1 \). By the Chinese Remainder Theorem this group is cyclic, isomorphic to \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]\cdot[[n]]} \). How many elements \( (x,y) \) generate the entire group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Since \( \gcd([[m]],[[n]]) = 1 \), \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \cong \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]\cdot[[n]]} \), a cyclic group of order \( [[m]]\cdot[[n]] \) with \( \varphi([[m]]\cdot[[n]]) = \varphi([[m]])\varphi([[n]]) = [[answer]] \) generators.
distractor_rationales: This is the size of the whole group, not the number of generators.
This wrongly assumes every non-identity element is a generator.
This drops the prime-power factors from each Euler totient.
This adds the group orders instead of multiplying the totients.
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[m]]\cdot[[n]]) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[m]]\cdot[[n]]) \).
